policy_statement: Require building energy audits for the largest and least efficient commercial and multifamily buildings (by 2015).
mechanism_description: It requires energy audits in large, inefficient commercial and multifamily buildings to identify cost-effective efficiency improvements, creating information that supports energy-saving actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (comfort/operations) → information disclosure (audit) → efficiency upgrades/operational improvements → ↓ energy input → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Building owners/managers of large or inefficient commercial and multifamily properties are required to assess energy performance via audits, which influences investment and operational decisions. (2) Mediating physical process: Identification and subsequent implementation of efficiency measures (e.g., HVAC tuning/replacement, controls, envelope improvements, lighting) reduces the energy required to deliver the same services. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower site energy use reduces upstream power-sector emissions and/or on-site combustion emissions, decreasing total GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption (electricity and/or fossil fuels) leading to lower GHG emissions from energy generation and on-site combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: audits → information/market signal → increased efficiency actions → reduced energy consumption → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the core lever is using less energy for the same building services (efficiency), which is Resource Efficiency; emissions reductions are downstream of reduced energy input rather than the policy directly mandating fuel switching or renewables.  
3) Edge cases applied: if an audit program is paired with mandatory electrification or renewable procurement, mitigation via fuel switching could become dominant—but that is outside this mechanism as defined (audit-driven efficiency signal).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy whose mechanism is “require audits to create an efficiency market signal” primarily targets efficiency opportunity identification and uptake, so the dominant effect remains reduced energy input.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether benefits accrue mainly via operational improvements vs capital retrofits, and whether impacts are mostly electricity vs gas, must be assigned using policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If audits are purely informational with very low subsequent action rates, realized mitigation may be small, but the intended dominant pathway remains efficiency-driven energy reduction; if audits are explicitly used to enforce mandatory retrofits or fuel switching, classification may shift depending on what requirement dominates.
key_indicators: "Require building energy audits"; "largest and least efficient commercial and multifamily buildings"; "help identify cost effective improvements"
co_benefits: Lower operating costs; improved indoor comfort; reduced local air pollution
instance_edge_case_notes: Audit requirements are a mandate, but the mechanism is primarily information generation/diagnosis (not a required upgrade), so it is treated as information-disclosure with enabling directness.
